museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Texas Instruments exits home computers, Spy Hunter becomes an arcade favourite, Atic Atac and late Spectrum releases crowd the UK conversation, and Christmas pressure tightens around home micros.
museum.curatorNote: November 1983 is built from late-year market anchors and release-window context. Where a title is simply late-1983, the file says so rather than inventing a day.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: November is where the home-computer boom shows both strength and casualties.

items.title: Texas Instruments exits the home-computer market
items.body: After severe losses and price-war damage, TI withdrew from the home-computer business, ending the TI-99/4A's mass-market push.
items.sourceNote: TI-99/4A histories place market exit in late 1983, often November.

items.title: Spy Hunter becomes a late-year arcade signal
items.body: Bally Midway's car-combat cabinet turned a driving game into a spy fantasy with weapons, boats and Peter Gunn swagger.
items.sourceNote: Spy Hunter references list 1983; some manual/material records cluster in late 1983.

items.title: Ultimate's late-year Spectrum run grows
items.body: Atic Atac and the studio's rapid catalogue made Ultimate feel like a label worth tracking, not just a single-game author.
items.sourceNote: Atic Atac and Lunar Jetman are both late-1983 Ultimate releases; exact month varies by source.

items.title: UK Christmas buying pressure intensifies
items.body: Magazine gift advice, machine shortages and cassette adverts shaped what a family could imagine owning.
items.sourceNote: UK seasonal hardware context, especially Electron/Spectrum/C64.

items.title: The console crash no longer explains everything
items.body: By November, the strongest games conversation was split across arcades, computers and Japan's new console market.
items.sourceNote: Synthesis from late-1983 release and hardware anchors.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight late-year releases and shelf objects.

items.title: Spy Hunter
items.body: A weaponised driving game with a spy-film mood and cabinet presence.
items.sourceNote: Spy Hunter references list 1983; exact month not universally stated.

items.title: Atic Atac
items.body: Ultimate's room-by-room maze adventure became another defining British micro artefact.
items.sourceNote: Atic Atac references list 1983; month varies.

items.title: Lunar Jetman
items.body: Ultimate expanded the Jetpac universe with a more ambitious sequel.
items.sourceNote: Lunar Jetman references list 1983; exact month not asserted.

items.title: Track & Field
items.body: Still new enough to turn arcade play into visible exertion.

items.title: Ant Attack
items.body: Quicksilva's isometric survival game belongs to the late-1983 Spectrum shelf.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: A Christmas-list candidate and schoolyard reference point.

items.title: Ultima III: Exodus
items.body: A deeper, slower computer game for players who wanted a world rather than a score.

items.title: Famicom early catalogue
items.body: Nintendo's Japanese machine was still building its identity far from UK high-street shelves.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: November's machines are winners, casualties and Christmas hopes.

items.title: TI-99/4A
items.body: A casualty of the price war, remembered as much for what the market did to it as for what it could do.

items.title: Acorn Electron
items.body: A desirable UK Christmas machine whose launch promise met supply constraints.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: The strongest local games signal for many British players: cheap tapes, recognisable hits and fast-moving publishers.

items.title: Arcade driving cabinets
items.body: Spy Hunter and Pole Position showed driving games branching into fantasy and spectacle.
items.sourceNote: Spy Hunter and Pole Position context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: November magazines looked like catalogues of possible presents.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Arcade favourites after the crash
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for November 1983.
items.curatorNote: The US game press had to cover play while the business story kept darkening.
items.body: The US game press had to cover play while the business story kept darkening.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Ultimate, Spectrum and arcade action
items.curatorNote: For UK readers, the strongest story could be a cassette label rather than an American console headline.
items.body: For UK readers, the strongest story could be a cassette label rather than an American console headline.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The Christmas micro decision
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for November 1983.
items.curatorNote: A home computer could still be sold as education, utility and games in one box.
items.body: A home computer could still be sold as education, utility and games in one box.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Late-year Spectrum hits
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for November 1983.
items.curatorNote: Spectrum readers had enough commercial games to need taste, not just availability.
items.body: Spectrum readers had enough commercial games to need taste, not just availability.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: November's network was a Christmas intelligence service.

items.title: Gift advice was infrastructure
items.body: Magazine recommendations helped families turn desire into a purchase.

items.title: Schoolyard reviews were fast
items.body: A tape could be judged by lunchtime if someone had loaded it the night before.

items.title: Arcade techniques were embodied
items.body: Spy Hunter knowledge included how the controls felt, not just what to press.

items.title: Machine shortages became rumours
items.body: Whether an Electron or C64 was obtainable could matter as much as specifications.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: November felt crowded, anxious and still playful.

items.title: Some companies vanished from wish lists
items.body: TI's exit made the market feel less innocent.

items.title: Ultimate felt like a badge
items.body: Following a studio was becoming part of UK gaming identity.

items.title: Arcades still gave spectacle
items.body: Spy Hunter made a cabinet feel like a chase scene.

items.title: Christmas raised the stakes
items.body: Choosing the wrong machine felt expensive and permanent.
